--- format: revealjs: theme: ../../styles/meds-slides-styles.scss slide-number: true chalkboard: true title-slide: false jupyter: eds232-env --- ## {#title-slide data-menu-title="Title Slide" background="#053660"} [EDS 232]{.custom-title}
[Lesson 3]{.custom-subtitle} [*Simple linear regression*]{.custom-subtitle} --- ## {#in-this-lesson data-menu-title="In this lesson"} [In this lesson]{.slide-title}

- The simple linear regression (SLR) model - Estimating coefficients with least squares - Standard errors and confidence intervals - Hypothesis testing: $t$-statistic and $p$-values - Assessing model accuracy: RSE and $R^2$ --- ## {#why-linear-regression data-menu-title="Why study linear regression?"} [Why study linear regression?]{.slide-title}
Regression estimates relationships between predictors and a response. It can help answer questions such as:
1. Is there a relationship between the predictor(s) and the response? 2. How strong is that relationship? 3. Which predictors are actually associated with the response? 4. How large is the effect of each predictor on the response? 5. Is the relationship linear?
. . . ::: {.center-text .body-text-m .teal-text} Linear regression supports both **inference** and **prediction**. ::: --- ## {#section-example data-menu-title="# A guiding example #" background="#047C90"}

A guiding example

--- ## {#greenabate data-menu-title="The GreenAbate dataset"} [Our example dataset]{.slide-title}
A research consortium tracks **200 manufacturing firms**. For each firm, they record annual spending on three emissions-reduction strategies and the resulting CO₂ savings:
::: {.body-text-m} - **`process`** — investment in cleaner production processes (\$K/year) - **`efficiency`** — investment in energy efficiency upgrades (\$K/year) - **`offsets`** — carbon offset purchases (\$K/year) - **`co2_reduction`** — annual CO₂ emissions reduced (ktCO₂e/year) :::
The researchers want to understand: ::: {.center-text .body-text-m .teal-text} *Do these investments actually drive emissions reductions, and if so, by how much?* ::: --- ## {#greenabate-plots data-menu-title="GreenAbate: data overview"} [CO₂ reduction vs. each predictor]{.slide-title}
::: {#4ac7eaca .cell execution_count=2} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](3_1_simple_linear_regression-SLIDES_files/figure-revealjs/cell-3-output-1.png){width=1187 height=390 fig-align='center'} ::: ::: ::: {.center-text .body-text-s} *Synthetic data generated for educational purposes only* ::: . . . `process` spending: strongest linear association with CO₂ reductions. We will use this predictor as our variable today and go back to the others in the next lesson. --- ## {#section-slr data-menu-title="# Simple linear regression #" background="#047C90"}

Simple linear regression

--- ## {#slr-model data-menu-title="The SLR model"} [The simple linear regression model]{.slide-title}
**Simple linear regression (SLR)** assumes a linear relationship between a single predictor $X$ and a response $Y$: $$Y \approx \beta_0 + \beta_1 X$$ . . . ::: {.body-text-m} - $\beta_0 =$ **$y$-intercept**: expected value of $Y$ when $X = 0$ - $\beta_1 =$ **slope**: average change in $Y$ for a one-unit increase in $X$ ::: . . .
Since $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$ are unknown, we **estimate** them from data: $$\hat{y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 x = \hat{f}(x)$$ --- ## {#slr-greenabate data-menu-title="SLR: fitted line"} [Using a linear model for our data]{.slide-title}
Using `process` as the sole predictor, a linear model for our data would assume this functional form: $$\texttt{co2_reduction} \approx \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 \cdot \texttt{process}$$ ::: {#9857dcc7 .cell execution_count=3} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](3_1_simple_linear_regression-SLIDES_files/figure-revealjs/cell-4-output-1.png){width=789 height=340 fig-align='center'} ::: ::: --- ## {#linear-model-checkin data-menu-title="Check-in"} [Check-in]{.slide-title}
:::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.teal-text} - How would you interpret the value of $\hat{\beta}_0$? - How would you interpret the value of $\hat{\beta}_1$? - If a firm spends \$150K on process upgrades, how would you calculate the predicted CO₂ reduction? ::: ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {#afbab7fd .cell execution_count=4} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](3_1_simple_linear_regression-SLIDES_files/figure-revealjs/cell-5-output-1.png){width=709 height=390 fig-align='center'} ::: ::: ::: :::: --- ## {#linear-model-checkin-answers data-menu-title="Check-in Answers"} [Check-in ]{.slide-title}
:::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.teal-text} - How would you interpret the value of $\hat{\beta}_0$? - How would you interpret the value of $\hat{\beta}_1$? - If a firm spends \$150K on process upgrades, how would you calculate the predicted CO₂ reduction? :::
- $\hat{\beta}_0 \approx$ 7\.70 ktCO₂e: predicted CO₂ reduction when a firm spends \$0 on process upgrades (baseline). - $\hat{\beta}_1 \approx$ 0\.0460 ktCO₂e per \$K: each additional \$1K in process spending is associated with 0\.0460 ktCO₂e of additional reduction. - Just plug in 150 into the line equation! ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {#7d7ddc97 .cell execution_count=5} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](3_1_simple_linear_regression-SLIDES_files/figure-revealjs/cell-6-output-1.png){width=709 height=390 fig-align='center'} ::: ::: ::: :::: --- ## {#section-estimation data-menu-title="# Estimating the coefficients #" background="#047C90"}

Estimating the coefficients

--- ## {#residuals-def data-menu-title="Residuals and RSS"} [Residuals and the RSS]{.slide-title}
Given $n$ training observations $(x_1, y_1), \ldots, (x_n, y_n)$, we want to find $\hat{\beta}_0$ and $\hat{\beta}_1$ such that the line $\hat{y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 x$ is as close as possible to the observed data. . . . The **residual** for observation $i$ is the difference between observed and predicted: $$e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i = y_i - \hat{\beta}_0 - \hat{\beta}_1 x_i$$ The **residual sum of squares (RSS)** totals all squared residuals: $$RSS = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{\beta}_0 - \hat{\beta}_1 x_i)^2.$$ . . .
::: {.center-text .body-text-m .teal-text} The **least squares** approach to fit the regression line chooses $\hat{\beta}_0$ and $\hat{\beta}_1$ to **minimize** the RSS. ::: --- ## {#residuals-plot data-menu-title="Residuals: visual"} [Residuals can be visualized as the vertical distance to the fitted line]{.slide-title}
::: {#0ff58c48 .cell execution_count=6} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](3_1_simple_linear_regression-SLIDES_files/figure-revealjs/cell-7-output-1.png){width=789 height=390 fig-align='center'} ::: ::: --- ## {#ls-formulas data-menu-title="Least squares formulas"} [Least squares solution]{.slide-title}
Minimizing the RSS using calculus gives the following formulas:
$$\hat{\beta}_1 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$$
$$\hat{\beta}_0 = \bar{y} - \hat{\beta}_1 \bar{x}$$ . . .
- The estimates depend entirely on the training data: if you feed the model different data, you will get different estimates. This is the source of estimation uncertainty. - In practice, **the computer computes these** for you. --- ## {#section-accuracy data-menu-title="# Coefficient accuracy #" background="#047C90"}

Accuracy of the coefficient estimates

--- ## {#standard-errors data-menu-title="Standard errors"} [Standard errors]{.slide-title}
Our true function describing the relationship between predictor and response is given by $f(X) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X$. Our observed data is described by: $$Y = f(X) + \epsilon = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X + \epsilon,$$ The **standard error** quantifies how far $\hat{\beta}_i$ is from the true $\beta_i$ on average. The standard error for the slope $\hat{\beta}_1$ is: $$SE(\hat{\beta}_1)^2 = \frac{\sigma^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$$ The standard error of the $y$-intercept $\hat{\beta}_0$ is: $$SE(\hat{\beta}_0)^2 = \sigma^2 \left[\frac{1}{n} + \frac{\bar{x}^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}\right]$$ ::: {.body-text-s} $\sigma^2 = \text{Var}(\epsilon)$ is the error variance (unknown in practice — estimated from the data). ::: . . . **Check-in:** In these formulas, what do $n$, $x_i$, $\bar{x}$ represent? --- ## {#confidence-intervals data-menu-title="Confidence intervals"} [Confidence intervals]{.slide-title}
Standard errors allow us to construct confidence intervals for the true coefficients. A **95% confidence interval** is a range of values such that, *if we repeated our experiment many times*, approximately 95% of the computed intervals would contain the true parameter value. A 95% confidence interval for a coefficient $\beta_i$ is: $$[\hat{\beta}_i - 2 \cdot SE(\hat{\beta}_i),\;\; \hat{\beta}_i + 2 \cdot SE(\hat{\beta}_i)]$$ ::: {.body-text-s} *(the factor 2 is approximate, the exact value comes from the $t$-distribution with $n-2$ degrees of freedom)* ::: . . .
::: {.body-text-s} **Interpretation:** > a 95% CI for $\beta_1$ of $[a_1, b_1]$ means that, using a procedure that captures the true slope 95% of the time, we estimate that a one-unit increase of $x$ is associated with an average increase in $y$ between $a_1$ and $b_1$ units. > a 95% confidence inerval for $\beta_0$ is $[a_0, b_0]$ means that, using a procedure that captures the true intercept 95% of the time, we estimate the model's $y$-intercept is between $a_0$ and $b_0$. ::: --- ## {#ci-greenabate-checkin data-menu-title="CIs: GreenAbate"} [Check-in]{.slide-title}
:::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} The exact 95% confidence intervals for each coefficient are: ::: {#0433b588 .cell execution_count=7} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` 95% CI for β₀ (intercept): [6.823, 8.567] 95% CI for β₁ (process): [0.041, 0.051] ``` ::: :::
::: {#62e42004 .cell execution_count=8} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](3_1_simple_linear_regression-SLIDES_files/figure-revealjs/cell-9-output-1.png){width=709 height=390 fig-align='center'} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} :::{.teal-text} 1. What does the CI [0\.041, 0\.051] tell us about the relationship between process upgrade spending and CO₂ reductions? 2. What if the interval had been [-0.002, 0\.051] instead? ::: ::: {.body-text-s} **Interpretation:** > a 95% CI for $\beta_1$ of $[a_1, b_1]$ means that, using a procedure that captures the true slope 95% of the time, we estimate that a one-unit increase of $x$ is associated with an average increase in $y$ between $a_1$ and $b_1$ units. > a 95% confidence inerval for $\beta_0$ is $[a_0, b_0]$ means that, using a procedure that captures the true intercept 95% of the time, we estimate the model's $y$-intercept is between $a_0$ and $b_0$. ::: ::: ::: --- ## {#ci-greenabate-checkin-solution data-menu-title="CIs: GreenAbate"} [Check-in]{.slide-title}
:::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} The exact 95% confidence intervals for each coefficient are: ::: {#37c6850b .cell execution_count=9} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` 95% CI for β₀ (intercept): [6.823, 8.567] 95% CI for β₁ (process): [0.041, 0.051] ``` ::: :::
::: {#ab99f32c .cell execution_count=10} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](3_1_simple_linear_regression-SLIDES_files/figure-revealjs/cell-11-output-1.png){width=709 height=390 fig-align='center'} ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} :::{.teal-text} 1. What does the CI [0\.041, 0\.051] tell us about the relationship between process upgrade spending and CO₂ reductions? 2. What if the interval had been [-0.002, 0\.051] instead? ::: ::: {.body-text-s} **Interpretation:** - Each additional \$1K in process spending is associated with a reduction of between 0\.041 and 0\.051 ktCO₂e per year on average. - Also, the entire interval [0\.041, 0\.051] is above 0 so our procedure points to a positive slope: firms that invest more in cleaner production processes achieve greater CO₂ reductions. - An interval of [-0.002, 0\.051] includes 0. We cannot rule out that $\beta_1 = 0$, meaning we cannot confidently conclude that process spending drives reductions. Wider intervals generally arise from smaller samples or noisier data. ::: ::: ::: --- ## {#section-hypothesis data-menu-title="# Hypothesis testing #" background="#047C90"}

Hypothesis testing

--- ## {#hypotheses data-menu-title="Null and alternative hypotheses"} [Is there a relationship between $X$ and $Y$?]{.slide-title}

- **Null hypothesis** $H_0$: no relationship between $X$ and $Y$. In mathematical terms this means $\beta_1 = 0$: $$\hat{Y} = \hat{\beta}_0.$$ - **Alternative hypothesis** $H_a$: there is some relationship between $X$ and $Y$. In mathematical terms this means $\beta_1 \neq 0$: $$\hat{Y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 X.$$ . . .
::: {.center-text .teal-text} To test $H_0$, we need to determine whether $\hat{\beta}_1$ is sufficiently far from 0 relative to its uncertainty. ::: --- ## {#t-stat data-menu-title="t-statistic and p-value"} [The $t$-statistic and $p$-value]{.slide-title}
The **$t$-statistic** measures how many standard deviations $\hat{\beta}_1$ is from 0: $$t = \frac{\hat{\beta}_1}{SE(\hat{\beta}_1)}.$$ ::: {.body-text-s} If there truly is no relationship between the variables ($\beta_1 = 0$), then $t$ follows a $t$-distribution with $n - 2$ degrees of freedom. :::
. . . The **$p$-value** is the probability of obtaining a $t$-statistic at least as large as the observed $|t|$ in absolute value, *assuming $H_0$ is true.* . . . ::: {.teal-text} :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Small } p\text{-value } (p < 0.05) \\ &\Rightarrow \text{unlikely to observe } |\hat{\beta}_1| \\ &\quad\text{this far from 0 by chance if } H_0 \text{ were true} \\ &\Rightarrow \textbf{reject } H_0 \\ &\Rightarrow \text{most likely } \beta_1 \neq 0 \\ &\Rightarrow \text{evidence of a relationship}\\ &\quad\text{between } X \text{ and } Y \end{aligned} $$ ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Large } p\text{-value} \\ &\Rightarrow \text{plausible to observe } |\hat{\beta}_1| \\ &\quad\text{this far from 0 by chance if } H_0 \text{ were true} \\ &\Rightarrow \textbf{fail to reject } H_0 \\ &\Rightarrow \beta_1 = 0 \text{ is consistent with the data} \\ &\Rightarrow \text{no evidence of a relationship}\\ &\quad\text{between } X \text{ and } Y \end{aligned} $$ ::: :::: ::: --- ## {#p-values-checkin data-menu-title="p-values: GreenAbate"} [Check-in]{.slide-title}
The table below shows the regression output for the SLR of `co2_reduction` on `process`: ::: {#f6060f56 .cell execution_count=11} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Coefficient Std. error t-statistic p-value Intercept 7.6953 0.4421 17.41 < 0.0001 process 0.0460 0.0026 17.64 < 0.0001 ``` ::: :::
:::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.teal-text} 1. What does the $p$-value for `process` tell us about the relationship between process upgrade spending and CO₂ reductions? 2. The intercept also has a very small $p$-value. What does this mean about the intercept estimate? What would be an equivalent $H_0$ for $\beta_0$? 3. How would you interpret the coefficient for `process` in plain language? ::: ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: ::: --- ## {#p-values-checkin-solution data-menu-title="p-values: GreenAbate"} [Check-in]{.slide-title}
The table below shows the regression output for the SLR of `co2_reduction` on `process`: ::: {#3f513053 .cell execution_count=12} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Coefficient Std. error t-statistic p-value Intercept 7.6953 0.4421 17.41 < 0.0001 process 0.0460 0.0026 17.64 < 0.0001 ``` ::: :::
:::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.teal-text} 1. What does the $p$-value for `process` tell us about the relationship between process upgrade spending and CO₂ reductions? 2. The intercept also has a very small $p$-value. What does this mean about the intercept estimate? What would be an equivalent $H_0$ for $\beta_0$? 3. How would you interpret the coefficient for `process` in plain language? ::: ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.body-text-s} 1. small $p$-value $\Rightarrow$ strong evidence that $\beta_1 \neq 0$: there is evidence of a positive relationship between process upgrade investment and CO₂ emissions reductions. 2. The $p$-value for the intercept tests $H_0: \beta_0 = 0$. A small $p$-value means the regression line does not pass through the origin. There is likely some baseline CO₂ reduction even at zero process spending. 3. On average, each additional \$1K invested in production processes is associated with ~0.046 ktCO₂e (46 metric tons) of additional CO₂ reduction. ::: ::: ::: --- ## {#section-model-accuracy data-menu-title="# Model accuracy #" background="#047C90"}

Assessing model accuracy

--- ## {#r2-def data-menu-title="R² statistic"} [$R^2$ statistic]{.slide-title}
$R^2$ measures the **proportion of variance in $Y$ explained by the model.** . . . To compute it we need these ingredients: - $TSS = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2 =$ **Total Sum of Squares**: *how much do the observations vary from the mean $\bar{y}$: a way of measuring total variability in $Y$* . . . - Intuitively: $y_i = \hat{y}_i$ means a $y$-value is explained by the regression . . . - $RSS = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 =$ **Residual Sum of Squares**: *variability in $y$ unexplained by the regression*. So $$\underbrace{TSS}_{\text{total variability in }Y} - \underbrace{RSS}_{\text{unexplained by regression}} = \text{variability explained by regression}$$ . . . We use this to define $$R^2 = \frac{TSS - RSS}{TSS}.$$ --- ## {#r2-interpret data-menu-title="Interpreting R²"} [Interpreting $R^2$]{.slide-title}

::: {.body-text-m} - $R^2 \in [0, 1]$ always - $R^2$ close to 1 → model explains most of the variability → good fit - $R^2$ close to 0 → $X$ alone is not capturing what drives $Y$ - Could mean: non-linear relationship, more predictors needed, high irreducible noise ::: . . .
::: {.center-text .body-text-m .teal-text} Always look at your data: a high $R^2$ does not guarantee a good model and a low $R^2$ does not mean no relationship! ::: --- ## {#accuracy-table-checkin data-menu-title="Model accuracy: GreenAbate"} [Check-in]{.slide-title}
::: {.teal-text} For our SLR model of `co2_reduction` on `process`, we have that $R^2$ = 0\.611. How would you interpret the $R^2$ value in plain language? ::: {#9844f61b .cell execution_count=14} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](3_1_simple_linear_regression-SLIDES_files/figure-revealjs/cell-15-output-1.png){width=709 height=390 fig-align='center'} ::: ::: ::: . . . An $R^2$ of 0\.611 means that approximately 61\.1% of the variability in CO₂ reductions across firms is explained by a linear variation in process upgrade spending alone. The remaining ~38\.9% of variability is driven by other factors not included in this model. --- ## {#training-metrics data-menu-title="R² is a training metrics"} [$R^2$ is an in-sample metric]{.slide-title}
The $R^2$ is computed on the same data used to fit the model. This is an in-sample measures, analogous to the training MSE from the previous lesson. . . . ::: {.body-text-m} Remember: - In-sample measures can give an overly optimistic picture of how the model generalizes to new data. - **For inference goals**: use all available data to understand relationships between the variables - **For prediction goals**: hold out a test set, fit on training data only, evaluate test MSE on the held-out set. ::: --- ## {#train-test-slr data-menu-title="Train/test MSE for SLR"} [Train/test MSE for our SLR]{.slide-title}
::: {#b4600d7a .cell execution_count=15} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](3_1_simple_linear_regression-SLIDES_files/figure-revealjs/cell-16-output-1.png){width=1029 height=390 fig-align='center'} ::: ::: --- ## {#bias-variance-slr data-menu-title="SLR in the bias-variance framework"} [SLR in the bias-variance framework]{.slide-title}
SLR has only 2 parameters ($\hat{\beta}_0$, $\hat{\beta}_1$). It is a very rigid model:
::: {.body-text-m} - [**Low variance:**]{.teal-text} the fitted line generally changes little across different training samples drawn from the same population - [**Potentially high bias:**]{.teal-text} if the true $f$ is non-linear, a straight line will be systematically wrong regardless of how much data we collect ::: --- ## {#inference-vs-prediction data-menu-title="Inference vs. prediction"} [Inference vs. prediction]{.slide-title}

:::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} **Inference** - Goal: *understand* the relationship - Use all available data - Report $R^2$, RSE, CIs, $p$-values - Example: "Does process spending drive CO₂ reductions?" ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} **Prediction** - Goal: *forecast* accurately for new observations - Hold out a test set - Report **test MSE** - Example: "Given spending levels, predict a firm's CO₂ reduction" ::: :::: --- ## {#recap data-menu-title="Recap"} [In this lesson we covered]{.slide-title}
- **Standard linear regression model:** $Y \approx \beta_0 + \beta_1 X$, estimated as $\hat{Y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 x$ - Minimizing $RSS = \sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ gives us coeffient estimates $\hat{\beta}_0, \hat{\beta}_1$ - Standard errors quantify uncertainty in the coefficient estimates - Confidence intervals give us a high-confidence method to estimate the coefficients - We can use Hypothesis testing to figure out if there is a relationship between the variables - $p$-value: probability of observing results if there was no variable relation $(H_0)$ - small $p$-value → reject $H_0$ → evidence of relation - $R^2$: proportion of variance in $Y$ explained by the model
